content.full_text:
via Federal Express                                April 15, 1992


Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California  95814

Re:  Request for FPPC Written Response to Questions
     Regarding Potential Conflicts of Interest by Members of
     the City of Laguna Niguel City Council Participating in
     Decisions Concerning Settlement of Lawsuit in Which
     Both They and the City are Defendants

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

     The undersigned is the City Attorney of the City of Laguna
Niguel. The purpose of this letter is to request a written
response from the Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC) on
questions concerning whether four of the five members of the City
of Laguna Niguel City Council would have a conflict of interest
under the Political Reform Act of 1974 in participating in a
decision regarding the settlement of a pending lawsuit against
the City of Laguna Niguel in which they are also named
defendants.

     Below we set out the facts concerning this matter. Also, we
set out three specific questions regarding this factual situation
and the undersigned's initial response to each of the questions.

     The undersigned, during the week of March 30, 1992,
discussed this matter with John Wallace of the Legal Division.
Mr. Wallace gave the undersigned his thoughts regarding this
matter and provided copies of a couple of FPPC advice letters
concerning governing body members participating in decisions
concerning litigation in which they are involved. However, the
factual situation presented in those letters are different from
that presented in this matter. Mr. Wallace suggested that we
consider requesting written advice on this matter, and he
indicated that it would take approximately 21 working days to
prepare such a written response after the FPPC's receipt of the
written request.

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
April 15, 1992
Page 2
________________________________________________________________

FACTS

     The City of Laguna Niguel was formed on December 1, 1989.
The formation of the City had been approved by the voters at the
November 1989 general election.  Also, at that election, the
voters elected a five member City Council.  Elected as Council
members were Patricia Bates, Paul Christiansen, James Krembas,
Larry Porter and Tom Wilson.  One of the unsuccessful candidates
was Eddie Rose.

     Essentially, since the formation of the City, Mr. Rose has
continually criticized actions of the City in general, and in
particular, actions of Council members Bates, Krembas, Porter and
Wilson.  The criticism has been in the form of letters to the
editor of newspapers and in "hand outs" that he distributed
around the City, including leaving copies on car windshields
during City events.  In October 1990, Mr. Rose initiated a recall
against the four Council members.  That recall terminated in
March 1991, when he failed to obtain a sufficient number of
recall petition signatures against any of the four Council
members to qualify the recall for submission to the voters.

     In July 1991, Mr. Rose filed a claim against the City of
Laguna Niguel for $10 million alleging that the four Council
members had defamed him relative to certain activities of theirs
done in opposition to Rose's recall against them.  His claim was
rejected by operation of law.  On January 23, 1992, Mr. Rose
filed a Complaint entitled Eddie Rose v. City of Laguna Niguel;
Thomas W. Wilson; Linda S. Bayme; James R. Krembas; Jeanette M.
Krembas; Patricia C. Bates; Leon Bates; Larry A. Porter, Nena B.
Porter; Dennis A. Harris; Alfred Churzaaii; Laguna Niguel for Tom Wilson
#91579 et al.; Orange County Superior Court Case No. 67 95 37.
Mr. Rose alleges in his Complaint that he was defamed by the
various anti-recall activities alleged in the Complaint.  Mr.
Rose claimed compensatory damages and punitive damages in the
amount of $1 million.  Further, he alleged that "most named
defendants were and are employees of defendant City of Laguna
Niguel and performed the above-described acts during the course
and scope of their employment for the City of Laguna Niguel."
The defendants in the lawsuit are the City, the four Council
members, the campaign committee of one of the four Council
members, the wives of Council members Krembas and Porter who were
apparently serving as their campaign treasurers, Linda Bayme who
was serving as the campaign treasurer for Council member Wilson
and who was on the City's Public Safety Commission and Special

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
April 15, 1992
Page 3
________________________________________________________________


Events Committee, and Dennis Harris and Al Chiurzaai who were
apparently supporters of the anti-recall campaign and who also
respectively were on the City's Public Safety Commission and the
Senior Citizens Committee.

     The defense of the City was tendered to the Southern
California Joint Powers Insurance Agency ("SCJPIA") which is a
joint powers agency consisting of a number of cities to provide
joint legal defense and indemnification from tort claims.  SCJPIA
accepted the defense and retained the law firm of Wood & Morimoto
to defend the City.  The named individual defendants are each, at
this time, providing for their own defense.

     Wood & Morimoto, on behalf of the City, directed
correspondence to Mr. Rose's attorney basically stating that
there was no City involvement in the actions alleged in the
Complaint, and suggesting that if Mr. Rose immediately dismissed
the City from his lawsuit, the City might be willing to waive
litigation costs incurred in the defense of the City.  At the
same time, Wood & Morimoto propounded substantial discovery to
the plaintiff's attorney seeking the factual basis of the
allegations in the Complaint to the effect that the named
defendants were acting within the course and scope of their
employment with the City in taking the actions alleged in the
Complaint.  Mr. Rose's attorney responded to the above-mentioned
Wood & Morimoto letter and discovery requests by proposing to
dismiss the City in return for the City waiving litigation costs
and malicious prosecution claims.

     The settlement of Mr. Rose's lawsuit against the City with a
waiver by the City of defense costs and malicious prosecution
costs requires approval by the City Council.  The proposed
settlement has raised the question of whether the City Council
should approve the proposed settlement, reject it, or approve a
counter-proposal such as the City agreeing to a waiver of
litigation costs and malicious prosecution claims in return for
Mr. Rose paying part of the costs incurred by the City.

QUESTIONS

     1.  Participating in Decisions Regarding Settlement of Rose
v. City of Laguna Niguel.

     The primary question presented is whether the four Council
members, who are named as individual defendants in the lawsuit,

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
April 15, 1992
Page 4
________________________________________________________________


may participate in the decision regarding the proposed settlement
of the lawsuit by Mr. Rose against the City?

     In approaching this issue, we attempted to ascertain whether
the four Council members have an economic interest on which a
decision regarding the settlement of the Rose lawsuit against the
City would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect. Of the various potential economic interests
(investments, interest in real property, sources of income,
business positions and gifts) the one that appears to pertain to
this situation is whether a decision might increase or decrease
the personal expenses, assets, or liabilities of the individual
Council members by $250 or more. The question that we attempted
to answer was, more specifically, whether or not at this time
(i.e., the time the decision on the proposed settlement would be
made) the settlement of the case against the City (or the
rejection of settlement or approval of a counter-proposal) would
reasonably foreseeably increase or decrease the expenses of the
four Council members in defending themselves in the Rose lawsuit
by $250 or more. We attempted to ascertain such things as
whether, if the case were settled and one defendant, namely the
City and its attorneys, were removed from the lawsuit, might that
be a financial benefit to the remaining individual defendants.
It might make the lawsuit less costly to defend in terms of less
attorney time spent communicating with other attorneys, and less
costs of service of documents. Also, we attempted to ascertain
whether such a settlement might make it more costly for the
individuals to defend the lawsuit. Having the City and its
attorneys in the lawsuit might possibly result in some sort of an
overall cost savings by some sort of mutual sharing of defense
expenses. Conversely, if the lawsuit were not settled on behalf
of the City, it might be more expensive for the individual
defendants because of having to continue to deal with the City
and its attorneys, or it might be less expensive if some sort of
cost sharing arrangement might be accomplished.

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
April 15, 1992
Page 5
___________________________________________________________________


We came to the conclusion that it was not reasonably
foreseeable that a decision, whether it be settlement or
rejection of settlement, would have an impact of $250 or more on
the individual defendants.  First, it was simply too speculative
as to whether either the settlement or rejection of settlement
would increase or decrease the overall costs of defense.  To
analyze such a question, one would have to make guesses at
various intangible aspects of handling litigation, such as
whether, as a general proposition, more attorneys in a lawsuit
makes defense more expensive, or whether in this case any sort of
cost sharing is possible.  Second, with the costs of defense for
the individual defendants spread out over nine individuals with,
we understand, seven of them being represented by one law firm,
and two of them being represented by another law firm, it would
be impossible to ascertain whether total cost savings (or a loss)
would be as much as $250 or more per person. Therefore, the
undersigned's conclusion is that it was not reasonably
foreseeable at this time that participating in making a decision
on the proposed settlement of the subject case between Eddie Rose
and the City of Laguna Niguel would cause an increase or decrease
of $250 or more on the personal expenses, assets and liabilities
of individual Council members, and thus, they were not prohibited
from participating in the making of such a decision.

     Also, we understand that if, in fact, there would be a
conflict of interest on the part of the four City Council members
in participating in the decision regarding the proposed
settlement of the Rose case, then two of the Council members,
chosen by lot, could, under existing FPPC regulations, be used to
constitute a quorum of three of the five Council members, and as
part of that quorum, fully participate in the discussion and
decision regarding the proposed settlement.  We would appreciate
a confirmation of this understanding of the FPPC regulations.

     2.   Participating in Decisions Regarding Appointment of a
Settlement Committee.

     A couple of the city Council members have raised the
possibility of the Council appointing a citizens' committee to
review and make recommendations to the City Council regarding the
settlement of the lawsuit.

     Would participating in making a decision to appoint such a
committee and then participating in the appointment of the
committee members violate the conflict of interest provisions of

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
April 15, 1992
Page 6
________________________________________________________________


the Political Reform Act?

     It seems to the undersigned that the analysis would be the
same as that regarding the first question in that if such a
committee were established and its members appointed, and it made
recommendations regarding the settlement of the subject lawsuit,
the Council would have to act on those recommendations. I do not
believe the Council has the authority to delegate to such a
committee, the settlement of the lawsuit. Thus, ultimately the
Council would have to make the decision on the proposed
settlement. Therefore, the council would still be faced with the
analysis of determining whether or not accepting the committee's
recommendation, whatever the recommendation might be, would have
a reasonably foreseeable affect on their personal assets of more
than $250 or more.

3. Participating in Decisions Regarding the Defense and
Indemnification of Individual Council Members.

     Some of the Council members have asked whether the City is
obligated to provide them with a defense and indemnification as
named defendants in the Rose lawsuit? The plaintiff in his
Complaint does allege that most of the individual defendants,
without identifying which ones, were acting within the ires and
scope of their employment when taking the actions alleged in the
Complaint, namely making the various allegedly defamatory
statements. However, it also appears from the facts alleged that
the individuals were not acting within the course and scope of
their employment with the City, but, in fact, were acting
relative to their anti-recall campaigns. At this point, all of
the named individual defendants have undertaken their own
defense. However, it is very possible that one or more of the
individuals will tender their defense to the City. Ultimately,
the city council may be called upon to make a decision as to
whether or not to provide at city expense the defense and
indemnification of the four individual City Council members and
possibly other of the named defendants who were serving on
various City commissions and committees.

     Would it be a violation of the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act for the Council members to
participate in making a decision regarding the City paying for
their individual defense and indemnification in the Rose lawsuit?

     We understand that the FPPC has rendered various advice
regarding the City paying for

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
April 15, 1992
Page 7

letters to the effect that in situations where a governing body
member is named as a defendant in a lawsuit, he is not prohibited
by the Political Reform Act from participating in decisions
regarding whether the public agency providing, at its expense, a
defense and indemnification from the claims alleged in the
lawsuit. Further, we understand that this advice is based on the
FPPC's application of Regulation No. 18700(d)(3). That
regulation specifically excludes from the definition of
"decisions", in which public employees are prohibited from
participating, decisions regarding the terms and conditions of
their employment. Decisions regarding whether a public entity
would provide defense and indemnification are considered by the
FPPC to be decisions regarding the terms and conditions of the
elected official's employment.

Relative to this question, we request a confirmation that
the FPPC would consider that the participation by the four
Council members in a decision regarding their indemnification and
defense would not be in violation of the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION

We request your written response to each of the three
questions set out above. Time is of the essence regarding this
matter. Presently, the settlement proposal described in this
letter is still pending. The case is not set for trial, but with
the various "fast track" procedures that have been instituted by
the courts for expediting litigation, this matter does need to be
resolved fairly soon (1) to avoid costs of litigation that would
be saved if the settlement is effectuated, or (2) to undertake
necessary defense activities if no settlement is achieved. We
would appreciate it if you could provide us with some idea as to
the timing of a response to this letter.

If you require any additional information or clarification
regarding the request contained in this letter, please do not
hesitate to contact the undersigned.

Yours very truly,

By [signature]
Terry E. Dixon
City Attorney for the
City of Laguna Niguel

TED:dv

costs of service of documents. Also, we attempted to ascerlain
whether such a settlenent might nake it more coslIy for the
individuals to defend the lavsuit. Having the City and its
attorneys in the lawsuit niqht possibly result in some sort of an
overall cost savings by sone sort of nutual sharing of defense
expenses. Conversely, if the lawsuit were not settled on behalf
of the city, it night be rnore expensive for the j"ndivj.dual
defendants because of having to continue to deal lrith the City
and its attorneys, or it night be less expensive if some sort of
cost sharing arrangement might be accornplished.


Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Cornnission
ApriL 15, 199 2
lve came to the conclusion that it rras not reasonably
foreseeable that a decision, lrhether it be settlement or
rejection of settl,enent, would have an irnpact of $250 or more on
the individual defendants. First, it was sirnply too speculative
as to whether either the settlenent or rejection of settlement
would increase or decrease the overalL costs of defense. To
analyze such a question, one would have to make guesses at
various intangible aspects of handling litigation, 
such as
v/hether, as a general proposition, nore attorneys in a lawsuit
makes defense more expensive, or whether in this case any sort of
cost sharing is possible. Second, with the costs of defense for
the individual defendants spread out over nine individuals with,
we understand, seven of them being represented by one law firm,
and two of them being represented by another law f j,rm, it would
be irnpossible to ascertain whether total cost savings (or a loss)
would be as nuch as $250 or more per person. Therefore, the
undersigned,s concl-usion is that it was not reasonably
foreseeable at this tine that participatj-ng in naking a decision
on the proposed settlement of the subject case bet$/een Eddie Rose
and the City of Laguna Niguel would cause an increase or decrease
of $250 or more on the personal expenses, assets and liabilities
of individual Council nenbers, and thus, they were not prohibited
from participating in the rnaking of such a decision.
AIso, we understand that if, in fact, there would be a
conflict of interest on the part of the four city council rnernbers
in participating in the decision regarding the proposed
settlement of the Rose case, then two of the Council nembers,
chosen by lot, could, under exj.sting FPPC regulations, be used to
constitute a quorun of three of the five Council menbers, and, as
part of that quorurn, ful1y participate in the discussion and
decision regarding the proposed settlernent. We would appreciate
a confirrnation of this understanding of the FppC regulations.
2. 
Participatino in Decisions Reqardinq Appointrnent of a
Settlement Committee.
A couple of the City Council mernbers have raised the
possibility of the council appointing a citizens, cornmittee to

.Wou1d participating in naking a decision to appoint such a

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.

the Political Reforn Act?
It 6eens to the undersigned that the anaLysis rrould be the

the Council would have to act on those recornmendations. I do not

CONCLUSION: we request your lrritten response to each of the three
questions set out above. Tine is of the essence regarding this

embedding.first_500_words: via Federal Express April 15, 1992 Scott Hallabrin, Esq. Acting General Counsel Fair Political Practices Commission 428 "J" Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, California 95814 Re: Request for FPPC Written Response to Questions Regarding Potential Conflicts of Interest by Members of the City of Laguna Niguel City Council Participating in Decisions Concerning Settlement of Lawsuit in Which Both They and the City are Defendants Dear Mr. Hallabrin: The undersigned is the City Attorney of the City of Laguna Niguel. The purpose of this letter is to request a written response from the Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC) on questions concerning whether four of the five members of the City of Laguna Niguel City Council would have a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act of 1974 in participating in a decision regarding the settlement of a pending lawsuit against the City of Laguna Niguel in which they are also named defendants. Below we set out the facts concerning this matter. Also, we set out three specific questions regarding this factual situation and the undersigned's initial response to each of the questions. The undersigned, during the week of March 30, 1992, discussed this matter with John Wallace of the Legal Division. Mr. Wallace gave the undersigned his thoughts regarding this matter and provided copies of a couple of FPPC advice letters concerning governing body members participating in decisions concerning litigation in which they are involved. However, the factual situation presented in those letters are different from that presented in this matter. Mr. Wallace suggested that we consider requesting written advice on this matter, and he indicated that it would take approximately 21 working days to prepare such a written response after the FPPC's receipt of the written request. Scott Hallabrin, Esq. Fair Political Practices Commission April 15, 1992 Page 2 ________________________________________________________________ FACTS The City of Laguna Niguel was formed on December 1, 1989. The formation of the City had been approved by the voters at the November 1989 general election. Also, at that election, the voters elected a five member City Council. Elected as Council members were Patricia Bates, Paul Christiansen, James Krembas, Larry Porter and Tom Wilson. One of the unsuccessful candidates was Eddie Rose. Essentially, since the formation of the City, Mr. Rose has continually criticized actions of the City in general, and in particular, actions of Council members Bates, Krembas, Porter and Wilson. The criticism has been in the form of letters to the editor of newspapers and in "hand outs" that he distributed around the City, including leaving copies on car windshields during City events. In October 1990, Mr. Rose initiated a recall against the four Council members. That recall terminated in March 1991, when he failed to obtain a sufficient number of recall petition signatures against any of the four Council members to qualify the recall for submission to the voters. In July 1991, Mr. Rose filed a claim against the City of Laguna Niguel for $10 million alleging that the four Council members had defamed
